rules.warning: Speakers on LAST, off FIRST. Always. Powering on the monitors before the rest of the signal chain is ready can send loud transient pops through the speakers, which damages the drivers over time. During shutdown, turn monitors off before powering down the computer or power conditioner.

NEVER engage phantom power with a ribbon microphone connected. This can permanently damage the ribbon element. Phantom power (48V) should only be used with condenser microphones. The AEA ribbon preamp is specifically designed for ribbons and does NOT use 48V phantom.

rules.trigger: Driving preamps hard without fader bank
rules.warning: When you push preamps hard for saturation and harmonic excitement, the output can be hot enough to clip the A/D converters. Use the Little Labs fader bank (channels 1-8) to pad the signal in 3dB steps between the preamp output and the converter input. The Sabatron has 30dB of built-in pad so it doesn't need this. The Psytech is transformer-less and not designed for saturation.
The talkback mic runs through the FMR preamp and requires phantom power to be enabled. If talkback isn't working, check that phantom power is engaged on the FMR preamp first.

The Oberheim Matrix 1000s (mechanical noise), Yamaha SY-77 (high-frequency noise), and Sabatron tube preamp (barely audible hum) are left OFF by default because they introduce noise. Only power them on when you're actively using them, and turn them off when you're done.

rules.trigger: Mac Mini connections after laptop use
rules.warning: If someone used their own laptop before your session, they may have disconnected Mac Mini cables. Check that all connections are in place: Ethernet (internet), Lightning (storage + UAD), HDMI (screen), and USB (keyboard/mouse/MIDI). Reconnecting a cable usually solves the issue.
That microphone is too heavy for a lightweight stand. It can tip over and damage the mic (replacement cost: $3,000+). Please use the heavy tripod stand.
Plugging or unplugging XLR cables while the preamp gain is up can cause loud pops that damage speakers. Turn down the gain and monitor volume before connecting or disconnecting cables.
Wrapping cables tightly around stands or boom arms can kink and damage the internal wiring. Best practice: coil cables loosely in loops (over-under method) and secure with a velcro strap.

The Bantam (TT) patchbay uses Bantam/TT cables, which are smaller than standard 1/4" TRS cables. They look similar but are NOT interchangeable. Forcing a 1/4" cable into a Bantam jack will damage the jack. If the cable doesn't slide in easily, you're probably using the wrong type.
